title: Stemming: Finding the Root
concept: Stemming uses simple rules to chop off word suffixes (e.g. 'running' -> 'run'), reducing different forms of a word to a common base.

exercises.prompt: Why would a computer program want to cut the endings off of words?

options.text: To group similar words together by their root meaning.
To check if the spelling of the word is correct.
To translate words into a different language.

exercises.explain: Computers want to group words like 'jumping', 'jumps', and 'jumped' together. Stripping away the suffixes (like '-ing' or '-ed') makes it easy to see they all share the core word 'jump'.

cards.title: Word Shortcuts
cards.body: When search engines search for words, do they treat "run", "running", and "ran" as completely different things? Let's discover how computers cut down words to find their core meaning!

cards.title: What is Stemming?
cards.body: Stemming is a quick trick computers use to find the base of a word. By cutting off common endings like "-ing", "-ed", or "-s", it groups similar words together. For example, "jumped", "jumping", and "jumps" all become "jump".

cards.title: The Pruning Shears
cards.body: Think of stemming like pruning a tree. You trim away the extra leaves and small twigs (like '-ing', '-ed', or '-s') to reveal the main branch (the root word). It might not look pretty, but it shows the core shape!

cards.title: Stemming in Action
cards.body: If you search a website for 'cooking tips', a stemmer cuts 'cooking' down to 'cook'. This helps the computer show you search results that contain 'cook', 'cooked', and 'cooks' too, making sure you don't miss anything!

cards.title: Not Always Perfect!
cards.body: Many think stemming always results in real dictionary words. In reality, stemming tools often make up non-words! For instance, 'beauty' and 'beautiful' might both be chopped down to 'beauti'. It doesn't look right, but it works for matching!

cards.title: Before vs. After
cards.body: Look at how stemming trims different words down to the same shared root.

exercises.prompt: If you apply a stemmer to the words "walked" and "walking", what root word do they both turn into?
exercises.explain: Stemming cuts off common endings. 'Walked' and 'walking' are simplified to their root form, which is 'walk'.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Stemming always produces a correctly spelled dictionary word.
exercises.explain: Stemming uses simple, fast rules to chop off endings. It does not check a dictionary, which is why it sometimes leaves behind misspelled or partial words like 'studi' from 'studies'.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete this rule of text cleanup.
exercises.sentence: By cutting off suffixes, computers reduce different word forms to their basic [blank].
exercises.explain: Computers chop off suffixes to find 'stems' like 'play' or 'help', grouping related words together.

exercises.prompt: A computer turns the word 'arguing' into 'argu'. Which type of tool did this?

options.text: Option A: A smart dictionary program that knows the spelling of 'argue'.
Option B: A simple rule-based stemmer that cut off '-ing' without checking spelling.
Option C: A translator converting English into computer code.

exercises.explain: Option B is correct because the stemmer did not use a dictionary; it just cut off the ending of 'arguing' to get 'argu', which is not a real dictionary word.

exercises.prompt: You are building a super-fast search engine. Why does your stemming tool turn 'studies' into 'studi' instead of the real dictionary word 'study'?

options.text: It uses a huge dictionary to find the perfect definition.
It blindly chops off common endings using simple rules.
It translates the words into another language first.
It rearranges the letters to create new words.

exercises.explain: Stemming relies on fast, simple rules to chop off prefixes and suffixes. While it is incredibly quick, it doesn't understand language rules deeply, which is why 'studies' gets chopped down to 'studi' instead of 'study'.
